sections.title: Fats
sections.narration: 34g of fat. omega 3 is doing a lot of the work here. for dairy, the fat side can either add richness or drag the whole thing down.

sections.title: Carbs
sections.narration: 6.2g of carbs. sugar and glycemic index matter most here. for a dairy food, this is doing the right stuff.

sections.title: Proteins
sections.narration: 6.2g of protein. bioavailability and amino acid quality are both strong. useful protein can rescue a lot of weaker traits here.
sections.subtitleText: 6.2g of protein. bioavailability and amino acid quality are both strong. useful protein can rescue a lot of weaker traits here.

sections.title: Vitamins
sections.narration: vitamin b12 at 10% daily value, vitamin a at 22% daily value stand out most. that is barely moving the needle.

sections.title: Minerals
sections.narration: calcium at 10% daily value stands out most. useful, but still pretty limited.

sections.title: Pros
sections.narration: the upsides first: easy protein delivery for normal diets because dairy gets real credit for convenience. clear calcium identity because that is a core category strength. slots into meals very easily because practical versatility matters a lot for dairy.

sections.title: Cons
sections.narration: on the weaker side: not everyone tolerates dairy well because that is a real use-case limitation. fat quality varies wildly across dairy because some dairy is clean, some really is not. processing changes the category a lot because the label alone does not tell the whole story.

closing.summary: Cream Cheese does have a few real-world upsides. but the nutrition case is still weak and the downsides drag it down hard. best only when the taste or format matters more than getting the strongest dairy profile.
